title: IR-1/CR-1 Spouse Visa Journey
titleUr: آئی آر-۱/سی آر-۱ شریک حیات ویزا کا سفر
description: Your complete guide to bringing your spouse to the United States on an IR-1 or CR-1 immigrant visa.
descriptionUr: آئی آر-۱ یا سی آر-۱ تارک وطن ویزا پر اپنے شریک حیات کو ریاستہائے متحدہ لانے کے لیے آپ کی مکمل رہنمائی۔

stages.name: Stage I: USCIS Petition Filing
stages.description: File Form I-130 with USCIS to begin the IR-1 spouse visa process for your Pakistani spouse.

steps.name: Determine Eligibility

questData.title: Initial Eligibility Check
questData.subtitle: Complete these five thematic screens to confirm you meet the core requirements before filing.

quests.title: Petitioner is a U.S. Citizen
quests.subtitle: Screen for petitioner's citizenship status and primary eligibility

screens.label: Petitioner Status
screens.question: What is the petitioner's current immigration status?
screens.helper: The IR-1 visa is only available when the petitioner is a U.S. citizen — not a green card holder.

options.label: U.S. citizen
Lawful Permanent Resident (green card holder)
options.sub: Spouse of LPR uses a different visa category (F2A), not IR-1.

options.label: Not sure
Other / none of these

screens.label: How Citizenship Was Obtained
screens.question: How did the petitioner become a U.S. citizen?
screens.helper: Each path to citizenship is valid for this petition. Pick the one that applies.

options.label: Born in the United States
Born abroad to a U.S. citizen parent (acquired or derived citizenship)
Naturalized (took the oath after immigrating)
Not sure

screens.label: Proof of Citizenship
screens.question: Does the petitioner have at least one accepted proof of U.S. citizenship?
screens.helper: USCIS accepts several document types. You do not need all of them — just one valid proof.

items: Valid or recently expired U.S. passport
U.S. birth certificate issued by a state or county
USCIS Certificate of Naturalization (Form N-550)
USCIS Certificate of Citizenship (Form N-560)
U.S. Consular Report of Birth Abroad (Form FS-240)

options.label: Yes — I have at least one of these documents
Not yet — but I can obtain one
No — and I'm not sure how to get one
Not sure

screens.label: Name Consistency
screens.question: Does the name on the citizenship document match the name the petitioner will use on this petition?
screens.helper: Name discrepancies are manageable with supporting documents. Flag this now so it isn't a surprise later.

options.label: Yes — name matches exactly
No — but I have legal proof of the name change
No — and I don't have name-change proof yet
Not sure

quests.resultLogic: (function() { if (answers['q1s1'] === 'lpr') return { state: 'stop', title: 'IR-1 requires a U.S. citizen petitioner', body: 'The IR-1 spouse visa is only available when the petitioner is a U.S. citizen. Lawful Permanent Residents may be eligible to petition under a different category (F2A preference visa). Consider speaking with an attorney about your options.' }; if (answers['q1s1'] === 'unsure' || answers['q1s1'] === 'other' || answers['q1s2'] === 'unsure') return { state: 'review', title: 'Petitioner\'s citizenship status needs clarification', body: 'The petitioner\'s status or citizenship path was marked as unclear. Confirm the petitioner\'s status before moving forward — this is the foundational requirement for the IR-1 petition.' }; if (answers['q1s3'] === 'no' || answers['q1s3'] === 'unsure' || (answers['q1s4'] && (answers['q1s4'] === 'diff_no_proof' || answers['q1s4'] === 'unsure'))) return { state: 'caution', title: 'Citizenship appears likely — but proof needs attention', body: 'The petitioner appears to be a U.S. citizen, but proof of citizenship or name-match documentation may need to be gathered or organized before filing. Address this early to avoid delays.' }; return { state: 'pass', title: 'Petitioner eligibility looks solid', body: 'The petitioner appears to be a U.S. citizen with identifiable proof. This core requirement is on track. Continue to the next section.' }; })()

quests.title: A legally valid marriage exists
quests.subtitle: Confirm there is a current, legally recognized civil marriage

screens.label: Relationship Status
screens.question: Which of these best describes the couple's current legal relationship?
screens.helper: USCIS requires an existing, legally recognized civil marriage — not just an engagement or ceremony.

options.label: Legally married (civil registration is in place)
Only engaged — not yet married
Religious or cultural ceremony only — no civil registration
options.sub: Civil registration is typically required for immigration purposes.

options.label: Separated or in the process of divorce
Not sure

screens.label: Legal Recognition
screens.question: Was the marriage legally recognized under the laws of the place where it occurred?
screens.helper: USCIS evaluates marriage validity under the law of the place where it was celebrated. Most civil marriages are valid, but some may have local registration gaps.

options.label: Yes — it was a legally registered civil marriage there
No — it was not legally registered or recognized there
Not sure

screens.label: Marriage Certificate
screens.question: Is there an official marriage certificate or record issued by the appropriate civil authority?
screens.helper: USCIS expects the official civil marriage certificate from the relevant government or registry office — not wedding photos or invitations.

items: Government-issued marriage certificate from the country or state where you married
Certified copy from a civil registry office
Apostille or official authentication if issued abroad

options.label: Yes — I have the official certificate
Not yet — but I can obtain a certified copy
No — and I'm not sure how to get one
Not sure

quests.resultLogic: (function() { if (answers['q2s1'] === 'engaged' || answers['q2s1'] === 'ceremony_only' || answers['q2s1'] === 'separated' || answers['q2s2'] === 'no') return { state: 'stop', title: 'A legally valid marriage is required for the IR-1', body: 'USCIS requires an existing, legally recognized civil marriage. Engagements, religious-only ceremonies, and ongoing separations or divorces do not qualify at the time of filing. Review the couple\'s current legal status.' }; if (answers['q2s1'] === 'unsure' || answers['q2s2'] === 'unsure') return { state: 'review', title: 'Marriage validity needs confirmation', body: 'The legal recognition of the marriage is unclear. This is a foundational requirement — confirm the marriage was properly registered under the applicable local law before proceeding.' }; if (answers['q2s3'] === 'can_get' || answers['q2s3'] === 'no' || answers['q2s3'] === 'unsure') return { state: 'caution', title: 'Marriage appears valid — certificate still needed', body: 'The marriage appears legally valid, but obtaining the official marriage certificate is an important early task. Start that process now — certified copies can take time, especially for international records.' }; return { state: 'pass', title: 'Marriage eligibility looks solid', body: 'A legally recognized civil marriage with official documentation appears to be in place. This requirement is on track.' }; })()

quests.title: All prior marriages were legally terminated
quests.subtitle: Confirm neither spouse was still married to anyone else when this marriage occurred

screens.label: Prior Marriage History
screens.question: Before this marriage, did either spouse have any previous legally registered marriages?
screens.helper: Count any marriage that was legally registered — anywhere in the world. Religious-only or unregistered ceremonies generally do not count.

options.label: No — neither spouse had any prior legally registered marriages
Yes — the petitioner had a prior marriage
Yes — the beneficiary had a prior marriage
Yes — both had prior marriages
Not sure

screens.label: Termination Timing
screens.question: Did every prior marriage end before the date of the current marriage?
screens.helper: A prior marriage must have ended first — by final divorce, annulment, or the death of the prior spouse — before the new marriage is legally valid.

options.label: Yes — all prior marriages ended before the current marriage
No — at least one prior marriage was still ongoing
Not sure about the exact timing

screens.label: Proof of Termination
screens.question: Is there documentation showing how each prior marriage legally ended?
screens.helper: USCIS will want to see this proof for every prior marriage — for both spouses.

items: Final divorce decree or judgment
Court-issued annulment order
Death certificate of the former spouse

options.label: Yes — documentation exists for all prior marriages
Some, but not all — documentation is missing for at least one
No — no documentation yet
Not sure

quests.resultLogic: (function() { if (answers['q3s1'] === 'none') return { state: 'pass', title: 'No prior marriages to verify', body: 'Neither spouse had a prior legally registered marriage, so this section is not an issue for your case. Well done — move on to the next section.' }; if (answers['q3s1'] === 'unsure') return { state: 'review', title: 'Prior marriage history needs clarification', body: 'It\'s unclear whether either spouse had a prior marriage. This should be confirmed — USCIS will require documentation if any prior marriages existed.' }; if (answers['q3s2'] === 'no') return { state: 'stop', title: 'Potential bigamy issue — legal review needed', body: 'If a prior marriage had not ended before the current marriage occurred, the current marriage may not be legally valid for immigration purposes. This is a serious issue that requires legal review before proceeding.' }; if (answers['q3s2'] === 'unsure') return { state: 'review', title: 'Termination timing is unclear', body: 'The timing of when prior marriages ended relative to the current marriage start date needs to be confirmed. This is a critical fact — consult an attorney if uncertain.' }; if (answers['q3s3'] === 'partial' || answers['q3s3'] === 'no' || answers['q3s3'] === 'unsure') return { state: 'caution', title: 'Prior marriages appear terminated — documents needed', body: 'The prior marriage(s) appear to have ended before the current marriage, but documentation is missing or incomplete. Gathering divorce decrees, annulment orders, or death certificates is an important early task.' }; return { state: 'pass', title: 'Prior marriage termination looks clear', body: 'All prior marriages appear to have legally ended before the current marriage, and documentation is available. This requirement is on track.' }; })()

quests.title: Marriage is bona fide
quests.subtitle: Screen for good-faith intent and evidence of a genuine relationship

screens.label: Genuine Intent
screens.question: When both spouses married, was it with the genuine intention to build a shared life together?
screens.helper: USCIS looks at whether the marriage was entered in good faith — not primarily for immigration purposes.

options.label: Yes — both intended a genuine marriage
No — immigration was the primary reason for the marriage
I'm not sure how to answer this

screens.label: Relationship Evidence
screens.question: Which of these types of relationship evidence do you currently have?
screens.helper: Select all that apply. Having multiple types of evidence across different categories strengthens the case significantly.

options.label: Shared address or co-habitation history
Joint finances, bank accounts, or shared bills
Photos, travel records, messages, or call logs
Children together, pregnancy, or family evidence
Statements from family, friends, or community who know the couple
None of the above yet

screens.label: Consistency
screens.question: Can both spouses describe the relationship consistently — timeline, how they met, living situation, and future plans?
screens.helper: USCIS interviews often test whether both spouses' stories match on basic facts. This is not about perfection, but about genuine shared knowledge.

options.label: Yes — both are comfortable and consistent on the basics
Mostly — there may be some minor gaps or differences
No — there are significant inconsistencies
Not sure

screens.label: Fraud Risk Flags
screens.question: Are there facts in this case that might raise questions about the relationship's authenticity?
screens.helper: This is just a flag — having a complicating factor doesn't mean the marriage isn't real. It means you'll want to address it proactively.

hint.intro: Common factors that may invite closer review:
items: Very short time between meeting and marriage (a few weeks)
Prior spousal petition filed by either spouse for a different person
Very limited documented contact or time spent together
Significant age difference with limited shared history
Prior finding of marriage fraud in either spouse's immigration history

options.label: No — no significant fraud-risk factors I can think of
Yes — there is at least one factor that may need explanation
Not sure

quests.resultLogic: (function() { if (answers['q4s1'] === 'no') return { state: 'stop', title: 'Good-faith intent is required', body: 'USCIS requires that the marriage was entered in good faith — not primarily to obtain immigration benefits. A marriage entered primarily for immigration is considered fraudulent. This is a serious issue that should be discussed with an attorney.' }; if (answers['q4s1'] === 'unsure' || answers['q4s4'] === 'yes' || answers['q4s4'] === 'unsure' || answers['q4s3'] === 'no') return { state: 'review', title: 'Potential bona fide marriage concerns', body: 'Some answers suggest this case may face closer scrutiny on bona fide marriage. This does not mean the marriage is invalid — but it means gathering strong, varied evidence early is important, and an attorney review is recommended.' }; const q4s2vals = answers['q4s2'] || []; if (q4s2vals.includes('none') || answers['q4s3'] === 'mostly' || answers['q4s3'] === 'unsure') return { state: 'caution', title: 'Marriage appears genuine — strengthen evidence early', body: 'The marriage appears to be in good faith, but the current evidence is thin or there are minor consistency concerns. Start building your evidence package now — across multiple categories.' }; return { state: 'pass', title: 'Bona fide marriage looks solid', body: 'Good-faith intent is affirmed, relationship evidence exists across multiple categories, and no major fraud-risk flags were identified. This section looks strong.' }; })()

quests.title: Additional possible visa ineligibilities
quests.subtitle: Early screening for common immigrant visa admissibility concerns

screens.label: Prior Immigration Actions
screens.question: Has the beneficiary ever been refused a U.S. visa, denied an immigration benefit, ordered removed, deported, or accused of using fraud or misrepresentation in any immigration matter?
screens.helper: Prior refusals don't automatically disqualify a case. But fraud or misrepresentation can be a serious bar to admissibility.

options.label: No — none of the above
Yes — at least one of these applies
Not sure

screens.label: Criminal History
screens.question: Does the beneficiary have any arrest, charge, or criminal conviction — anywhere in the world?
screens.helper: Even minor or older matters can affect immigration eligibility. Do not guess or omit — certain convictions can trigger bars to admissibility.

options.label: No — no arrests, charges, or convictions
Yes — there is at least one arrest, charge, or conviction
Not sure

screens.label: Health & Medical
screens.question: Is there any known health issue that might arise during the required immigration medical exam?
screens.helper: The beneficiary must pass a medical exam by a USCIS-designated physician. This screen only asks whether there's a known concern to plan around.

hint.intro: Medical grounds of inadmissibility can include:
items: Certain communicable diseases of public health significance
Failure to have required immigration vaccinations
Certain documented substance use disorders

options.label: No known health concern for the medical exam
Yes — there is a health concern that may come up
Not sure

screens.label: Prior U.S. Immigration History
screens.question: Has the beneficiary ever entered the U.S. without inspection, overstayed a visa, or accumulated unlawful presence in the United States?
screens.helper: Unlawful presence can trigger multi-year or permanent bars to admissibility depending on the amount of time and how departure occurred.

options.label: No — the beneficiary has no such U.S. immigration history
Yes — at least one of these applies
Not sure

screens.label: Financial Sponsorship
screens.question: Is there any concern that meeting the financial sponsorship requirements (Affidavit of Support) may be difficult?
screens.helper: This does not affect eligibility right now — it's a forward-looking flag to plan for. The petitioner will need to meet the minimum income threshold or secure a joint sponsor.

options.label: No known concern — petitioner can likely meet the income requirement
Yes — there may be a challenge meeting the income threshold
Not sure yet

quests.resultLogic: (function() { const r = ['q5s1','q5s2','q5s3','q5s4'].filter(k => answers[k] === 'yes' || answers[k] === 'unsure'); if (r.length > 0) return { state: 'review', title: 'One or more admissibility concerns flagged', body: 'This screening identified at least one area — prior immigration actions, criminal history, medical, or unlawful presence — that warrants closer legal review. These issues do not automatically disqualify the case, but they require attorney analysis before proceeding.' }; if (answers['q5s5'] === 'yes' || answers['q5s5'] === 'unsure') return { state: 'caution', title: 'Financial sponsorship may need planning', body: 'No admissibility red flags were identified. However, the financial sponsorship requirement may need attention. Review the current income thresholds and consider whether a joint sponsor may be needed.' }; return { state: 'pass', title: 'No significant ineligibility flags identified', body: 'Based on this early screening, no major admissibility concerns were flagged. Remember: this is not a legal determination. Always review the full case with a qualified immigration attorney.' }; })()

steps.notes: Petitioner must be U.S. citizen (not green card holder). Marriage must be legal and genuine.

steps.name: Gather Required Documents
steps.description: Prepare the documents needed for your IR-1 spouse petition. Open each item to see exactly what is needed, where to get it, and what to watch out for.

documentItems.name: Proof of U.S. Citizenship
documentItems.subtitle: Establishes the petitioner's right to file for an IR-1 spouse

documentItems.name: Beneficiary Passport
documentItems.subtitle: Pakistan passport of the immigrant spouse

documentItems.name: Nikah Nama (Original)
documentItems.subtitle: Islamic marriage contract — the foundational marriage document

documentItems.name: NADRA Marriage Certificate
documentItems.subtitle: Government civil registration of the marriage (MRC)

documentItems.name: Relationship Evidence
documentItems.subtitle: Proof the marriage is genuine and entered in good faith

documentItems.name: Prior Marriage Termination Proof
documentItems.subtitle: Required if either spouse had a previous marriage

documentItems.name: Translations
documentItems.subtitle: Certified English translations of non-English documents

documentItems.name: Birth Certificate
documentItems.subtitle: Beneficiary's Pakistan birth record — needed at consular stage

documentItems.name: Police Character Certificate
documentItems.subtitle: Pakistan PCC — required at consular stage

documentItems.name: Passport Photos
documentItems.subtitle: Required at consular and visa application stages

steps.pakistanSpecific: Union Council registration increasingly required by Islamabad Embassy. Police certificate: 3-7 days processing for Punjab/Islamabad.

steps.name: Choose Filing Method
steps.description: Choose how to file Form I-130 with USCIS. Both methods are fully accepted and take the same amount of time to process — select the one that works best for you.

filingData.title: Decide Filing Method
filingData.subtitle: Choose how to file Form I-130 with USCIS. Both methods are fully accepted and take the same amount of time to process — select the one that works best for you.

methods.title: PAPER FILING
methods.name: File by Mail
methods.subtitle: mail packet to USCIS
features: Pay by check or money order

filingData.footerNote: Fees as of March 2026 per the USCIS G-1055 fee schedule. Processing time is the same for both methods. Always verify at uscis.gov before filing.

screens.question: Is an attorney or accredited representative managing the filing for you?
screens.helper: If a representative is handling the paperwork, they typically decide the filing method — this walkthrough may not apply.

items: Attorneys can file I-130 online via their USCIS representative account, or by mailing a paper packet — both work
Ask your attorney or document preparer which method they prefer before deciding

options.label: Yes — a representative is handling this
No — I'm filing on my own
Not sure yet

screens.question: Can the petitioner create and manage a USCIS online account?
screens.helper: Online filing requires a free USCIS account at my.uscis.gov — used to complete the form, upload documents, and pay the filing fee.

items: Account setup takes a few minutes and just needs a valid email address
Your USCIS account also delivers notices, requests for evidence, and case updates
Paper filers can add their case to a USCIS account later using the mailed receipt number

options.label: Yes — comfortable setting up and using an online account
No — online accounts aren't practical right now
Not sure

screens.question: Are the required documents available in digital form for upload?
screens.helper: Online filing requires uploading scanned or photographed copies. Paper filing uses physical copies sent by mail.

items: PDF, JPG, or PNG are accepted — a phone camera usually works fine
Documents must be clear and fully legible
A flatbed scanner gives better results for dense documents like a Nikah Nama

options.label: Yes — documents are ready or easy to scan
No — physical copies only, hard to digitize right now
Not sure

screens.question: Does the petitioner have a preference for how to file?
screens.helper: Both methods are fully valid. Your comfort level is a legitimate deciding factor — there is no wrong choice.

options.label: Prefer online — convenient and saves $50
Prefer paper — more comfortable mailing a physical packet
No strong preference — happy with whatever works best

steps.name: Fill Form I-130
steps.description: Use Rahvana's guided Autoform Filler to complete the I-130 Petition for Alien Relative — with structured step-by-step support, clear prompts, and a built-in review flow.

toolData.title: Fill Form I-130
toolData.subtitle: Use Rahvana's guided Autoform Filler to complete the I-130 Petition for Alien Relative — with structured step-by-step support, clear prompts, and a built-in review flow.

launchModule.heading: Guided completion, start to finish

supportCards.label: Have these ready
items: Petitioner's citizenship proof
Beneficiary's passport details
Marriage certificate info
Addresses for the past 5 years

supportCards.label: Good to know
items: You can save progress and return
No fee is charged inside the tool
USCIS account setup is a separate step
Complete this before uploading to USCIS

launchModule.heading: Spouse beneficiary information, guided

supportCards.label: Have these ready
items: Spouse's full legal name and DOB
Addresses for the past 5 years
Employment history (last 5 years)
Passport and immigration info

supportCards.label: Good to know
items: Best completed right after Form I-130
Filed together as one petition package
You can save progress and return later
No fee is charged inside the tool

steps.name: Create USCIS Account
steps.description: Set up the petitioner's USCIS online account — the gateway for filing Form I-130 digitally, tracking the case, and responding to any USCIS requests.

toolData.title: Create USCIS Account
toolData.subtitle: Set up the petitioner's USCIS online account — the gateway for filing Form I-130 digitally, tracking the case, and responding to any USCIS requests.

launchModule.heading: Create your secure USCIS online account
launchModule.note: Opens USCIS account creation page in a new tab

secondaryActions.title: Step-by-Step Account Setup Guide
secondaryActions.subtitle: Walk through the USCIS account creation process screen by screen.

secondaryActions.title: Save USCIS Credentials
secondaryActions.subtitle: Securely store your USCIS login details in Rahvana's Portal Wallet.

toolData.disclaimer: Rahvana provides structured guidance only. This is not legal advice. Credentials are stored locally for your session only. Manage them in your Portal Wallet.

toolData.title: Start Form I-130 on USCIS
toolData.subtitle: Sign in to your USCIS account and begin the online I-130 petition. From your account dashboard, click "File a Form Online" to start the form.

launchModule.heading: Start the online I-130 petition on USCIS
features: Click "File a Form Online" on the dashboard
Select Form I-130 and begin the draft
Progress saves automatically as you go
launchModule.note: Opens your USCIS account in a new tab — return here after starting the form

toolData.title: Upload Forms & Evidence
toolData.subtitle: Attach your supporting documents inside the USCIS online portal. This is the evidence-upload phase.

launchModule.heading: Attach your evidence files to the I-130 filing
features: Sign in and open your I-130 draft
launchModule.note: Accepted formats: PDF, JPG, or JPEG only. Max 12MB per file.

steps.name: Pay Filing Fee
steps.notes: As of Oct 28, 2025: Checks/money orders NO LONGER accepted. Electronic payment only.

steps.name: Wait for Processing
steps.notes: RFE deadline usually 30-87 days. Respond promptly to avoid delays.

stages.name: Stage II: NVC/CEAC Processing
stages.description: Pay fees → Submit DS-260 → Upload documents → Documentarily Qualified

steps.name: Receive NVC Welcome Letter
steps.output: Case Number and Invoice ID obtained
steps.notes: Case Number starts with ISL for Islamabad

steps.name: Pay NVC Fees
steps.notes: Fees are non-refundable. Payment reflects in 1-2 business days.

steps.name: Complete DS-260 Form
steps.notes: Cannot change after submission except via NVC email. Save frequently!

steps.name: Prepare Civil Documents
documents: Marriage certificate (Nikah Nama + translation + Union Council)
steps.pakistanSpecific: Police certificate valid 1 year. Union Council registration increasingly required.

steps.name: Complete I-864 Affidavit
steps.notes: Minimum income: $25,550 for household of 2 (2025). Use joint sponsor if needed.

steps.name: Upload Documents to CEAC
steps.notes: Upload one at a time. Max 4 MB per file. PDF preferred.

steps.name: NVC Document Review
actions: Respond to any document requests immediately
steps.notes: Common rejections: poor scan quality, missing translations, expired police certificate

steps.name: Wait for Interview Scheduling
actions: DO NOT obtain new police certificate yet
steps.notes: Cannot speed up this process. Interview scheduled by embassy.

stages.name: Stage III: Medical + Interview
stages.description: Complete medical exam → Attend consular interview → Visa decision

steps.name: Schedule Medical Examination
steps.notes: Medical results valid 6 months. Schedule 2-3 days before interview ideally.

steps.name: Obtain Fresh Police Certificate
actions: Apply at Police Khidmat Markaz or online
Provide CNIC, passport, affidavit if needed
steps.notes: Get new if previous one is 6+ months old. Need ORIGINAL for interview.
steps.pakistanSpecific: Punjab Police Portal: pkm.punjab.gov.pk or islamabadpolice.gov.pk

steps.name: Gather Original Documents
steps.notes: Bring originals + copies. Organize petitioner and beneficiary documents separately.

steps.name: Attend Medical Exam
steps.notes: DO NOT OPEN envelope. Results ready in 2-5 days. Collect 1-2 days before interview.

steps.name: Prepare for Interview
actions: Arrange travel to Islamabad if needed
steps.notes: Be truthful, consistent. Know spouse details well. Dress professionally - no jeans/t-shirts.

steps.name: Attend Visa Interview
steps.pakistanSpecific: Address: Diplomatic Enclave, Ramna 5, Islamabad. NO mobile phones allowed.
steps.notes: Common questions: How did you meet? Tell me about your wedding. Where will you live?

steps.name: Track Status (If AP)
actions: If placed in AP, check CEAC weekly
Be patient - no way to expedite
steps.notes: AP duration: 2 weeks to 12+ months (unpredictable). Common for security clearances.

stages.name: Stage IV: Post-Interview & Travel
stages.description: Receive passport with visa → Pay USCIS fee → Travel to U.S.

steps.name: Receive Passport with Visa
actions: Verify: name, DOB, passport number, visa category (IR-1/CR-1)
steps.notes: MUST enter U.S. before visa expiration (typically 6 months from medical exam)

steps.name: Pay USCIS Immigrant Fee
actions: Create account at USCIS immigrant fee site
steps.notes: Pay BEFORE travel recommended. Green card mailed to this address within 90 days.

steps.name: Prepare for Travel
actions: Book flight with buffer before visa expiration
Pack carry-on: passport, sealed envelopes, confirmations
steps.notes: Sealed immigration packet MUST stay sealed. Pack in carry-on, never checked luggage.

steps.name: Understand Immigration Status
steps.notes: IR-1: 10-year green card. CR-1: 2-year conditional, must file I-751 to remove conditions.

stages.name: Stage V: U.S. Arrival
stages.description: Enter U.S. → CBP inspection → I-551 stamp → Green card delivery

steps.name: Arrival at Port of Entry
steps.notes: Common questions: Purpose? Where living? How long married?

steps.name: Secondary Inspection (If Needed)
steps.notes: Common for immigrant visa holders. Don't worry - routine.

steps.name: Receive I-551 Stamp
steps.output: I-551 stamp = temporary green card (valid 1 year)
steps.notes: This stamp proves permanent residence and work authorization until physical card arrives

steps.name: Clear Customs
actions: Declare items over $800 or restricted items
steps.notes: Declare any food, plants, amounts over $10,000 cash

steps.name: Post-Arrival Actions
actions: Wait for Social Security card (2-4 weeks)
If doesn't arrive, apply at SSA office
Register for Selective Service if male 18-25
steps.notes: SSN auto-issued if checked Yes on DS-260. Green card arrives within 90 days of entry or payment.

steps.name: Receive Green Card
actions: Track via USCIS immigrant fee account
steps.notes: If error on card, file I-90 immediately. IR-1: valid 10 years. CR-1: valid 2 years.

steps.name: Maintain Green Card
actions: Carry green card at all times
Update address with USCIS within 10 days (AR-11)
Don't leave U.S. for 6+ months without planning
For CR-1: File I-751 within 90 days of 2-year anniversary
steps.notes: CR-1 CRITICAL: Must file I-751 jointly with spouse to remove conditions. Failure = loss of status.

steps.name: Path to Citizenship (Future)
actions: After 3 years as LPR, can apply for citizenship via N-400
Requirements: physical presence, good moral character, English, civics test
steps.notes: Optional - can remain LPR indefinitely. Citizenship allows voting, more job options, cannot be deported.
